text: This opening chapter establishes Baker's core polemic and the architectural scaffolding the rest of the book depends on: rhythm strumming is declared obsolete and must be replaced by a systematic chord vocabulary that powers solos, intros, comping, and fills (Replacing strummed chords). Baker prunes guitar's chord universe down to a curated, numerically referenced set of 26 essential chords, dismissing the rest as 'meaningless,' and anchors initial study in two seed keys, G and C, from which all other keys are derived by transposition (The 26 essential chords; Two starting keys). The load-bearing rules introduced here are non-negotiable and recur throughout the method: transposition to every key is mandatory and is what defines mastery (Transposition is mandatory); the student must keep a hand-written music book to retain the work (Keep a music writing book); every new chord shape is internalized by Baker's signature chromatic up-the-neck drill (Chromatic chord exercise); lessons must not be skipped because the chord-numbering system is cumulative (Don't skip lessons); and rhythm comping itself is restricted to a tight six-chord palette (Chords No. 1-6) that suffices across the four foundational keys F, G, A flat, and B flat (Six core rhythm chords; Most important rhythm chords). The chapter also introduces the two-line standard-vs-new substitution format that becomes the dominant pedagogical device of the book (Standard vs. new chords).
